French Notes

From \"elearningfrench.com\"

Lesson 1

  • Definite articles:
    • Number Gender Before a noun beginning with a...
      consonant vowel

      Singular      		 Masculine le l’
      Feminine la l'
      Plural
      		 Masculine and feminine
      		 les
    • FrenchEnglish
      le restaurantthe restaurant
      la ruethe street
      l'enfantthe child
      les amisthe friends
  • Subject Pronouns:
    • TypeFrenchEnglish
      Singular je I
      tu you
      vous you
      il (masculine) he
      elle (feminine) she
      on one, we, people
      Plural nous we
      vous you
      ils (masculine) they
      elles (feminine) they
  • There are several ways of formulating questions in French. One way is to reverse the positions of the subject pronoun and the verb. This process is called inversion:
  • Vous avez des nouvelles. Avez-vous des nouvelles?
    Il est à Lyon maintenant. Est-il à Lyon maintenant?
    Nous allons au café. Allons-nous au café?
  • Note that when the 3rd person singular ends with a vowel, a \"t\" is added between the verb and the pronoun.
  • Il va bien.Va-t-il bien?
  • The \"t\" is pronounced as part of the syllable that follows it.
  • Question inversion is possible only with subject pronoun and verb. When the subject is a noun, the noun remains before the verb and the appropriate pronoun must be introduced after the verb to form a question.
  • FrenchEnglish
    Vos parents sont-ils toujours en vacances?Are your parents still on vacation?
    Le restaurant est-il bon?Is the restaurant good?
  • The most frequently occurring verb in French is être, to be. Here are its forms, which vary grammatically according to the noun or subject pronoun with which it is tied:
    • FrenchEnglish
      je suis I am
      tu es you are
      il est, elle est, on est he is, she is, one is
      nous sommes we are
      vous êtes you are
      ils sont, elles sont they are

Lesson 2

  • Indefinite articles:
    • 'une' occurs before feminine singular nouns.
    • 'un' occurs before masculine singular nouns.
    • 'des' is the plural form for the indefinite article.
  • FrenchEnglish
    ils ont un enfant they have a child
    ils ont des enfants they have children
    une chambre a bedroom
  • Cardinal numbers (1-19):
    • 1 un / une
      2 deux
      3 trois
      4 quatre
      5 cinq
      6 six
      7 sept
      8 huit
      9 neuf
      10 dix
      11 onze
      12 douze
      13 treize
      14 quatorze
      15 quinze
      16 seize
      17 dix-sept
      18 dix-huit
      19 dix-neuf
  • Negative Adverb:
    • A verb is negated by the presence of ne before it and pas after it.
    • Before a vowel, n' must occur instead of ne:
    • FrenchEnglish
      Lisez. Read.
      Ne lisez pas. Do not read.
      Est-il? Is he?
      N'est-il pas? Isn't he?
      Sont-elles? Are they?
      Ne sont-elles pas? Aren't they?
      Je ne suis pas. I am not
      Tu n'es pas / Vous n'êtes pas. You are not (singular)
      Il / Elle / On n'est pas. He / She / One is not.
      Nous ne sommes pas. We are not.
      Vous n'êtes pas. You are not. (plural)
      Ils / Elles ne sont pas. They are not.
      Ne suis-je pas? Am I not?
      N'es-tu pas / N'êtes-vous pas? Are you not? (singular)
      N'est-il (elle / on) pas? Is he (she / one) not?
      Ne sommes-nous pas? Are we not?
      N'êtes-vous pas? Are you not? (plural)
      Ne sont-ils (elles) pas? Are they not.
  • Verb avoir:
    • The second most frequently occurring verb in French is avoir (to have).
    • Subject Pronoun (SP) + Verb (V):
    • FrenchEnglish
      j'ai I have
      tu as you have (singular)
      il a (elle,on) he has (she, one)
      nous avons we have
      vous avez you have (plural)
      ils ont (elles) they have
    • Verb + SP (in questions):
    • FrenchEnglish
      ai-je ...? have I ...?
      as-tu ...? have you ...? (singular)
      a-t-il ...? (elle,on) has he ...? (she,one)
      avons-nous ...? have we ...?
      avez-vous ...? have you ...? (plural)
      ont-ils ...?(elles) have they ...?
    • SP + ne + V + pas:
    • FrenchEnglish
      je n'ai pas ... I don't have ...
      tu n'as pas ... you don' t have ... (singular)
      il n'a pas ... (elle, on) he doesn't have ... (she, one)
      nous n'avons pas ... we don't have ...
      vous n'avez pas ... you don't have ... (plural)
      ils n'ont pas ... (elles) they don't have ...
    • ne + V + SP + pas (in questions):
    • FrenchEnglish
      n'ai-je pas ...? don't I have ...?
      n'as-tu pas ...? don' t you have ...? (singular)
      n'a-t-il pas ...? (elle, on) doesn't he have ...? (she, one)
      n'avons-nous pas ...? don't we have ...?
      n'avez-vous pas ...? don't you have ...? (singular)
      n'ont-ils pas ...? (elles) don't they have ...?

Lesson 3

  • Demonstrative determiners:
    • Number Gender Before a consonant Before a vowel
      Singular Maculine ce cet
      Feminine cette cette
      Plural Masculine and Feminine ces ces
    • FrenchEnglish
      ce restaurantthis restaurant
      ce monsieurthis gentleman
      cet enfantthis baby
      cet hôtelthis hotel
      cette mallethis trunk
      cette amiethis friend
      ces restaurantsthese restaurants
  • The preposition à + definite article has several shapes:
  • Number Gender Before a consonant Before a vowel
    Singular Maculine au
    à l’
    Feminine à la
    Plural Masculine and Feminine aux aux
  • Au, à la, à l', aux have different equivalents in English:
    • To the
    • At the, in
    • The verb 'to be' followed by the preposition à has also the meaning of \"to belong to\"
    • FrenchEnglish
      Allez-vous à la gare?Are you going to the station?
      Je suis à l'hôtel.I am at the hotel.
      Aux guichets deux et trois.At ticket windows two and three.
      Il est au lit.He is in bed.
      C'est à la soeur de Janine.It belongs to Janine's sister.
  • Prefixing Est-ce que to the Subject-Verb type of utterance makes it into a yes/no question:
  • FrenchEnglish
    C'est 42 francs.It's 42 francs.
    Est-ce que c'est 42 francs?Is it 42 francs?
    Je peux vous donner deux coins fenêtres?I can give you two window seats.
    Est-ce que je peux vous donner deux coins fenêtres?Can I give you two window seats?

Lesson 4

  • In French, the possessive determiners (my, his, her, our, etc.) agree in gender and number with the noun which follows.
    • English / Anglais Gender / Genre Before a singular noun beginning with a vowel Before a singular noun beginning a consonant Before a plural noun
      My If noun is feminine mon ma mes
      My If noun is masculine mon
      His, Her, It's, One's If noun is feminine son sa ses
      His, Her, It's, One's If noun is masculine son
      Our Masculine or feminine notre nos
      Your Masculine or feminine votre vos
      Their, Theirs Masculine or feminine leur leurs
  • Du, de la, de l', des have different equivalents in English:
    • Some, any (indicating quantity)
    • From the
    • Possessive case
    • Number Gender Before a consonant Before a vowel
      Singular Maculine du de l'
      Feminine de la
      Plural Masculine and Feminine des des
  • Numbers to 100:
    • NumberFrenchNumberFrench
      20vingt21vingt et un
      30trente31trente et un
      40quarante41quarante et un
      50cinquante51cinquante et un
      60soixante61soixante et un
      70soixante-dix71soixanteet onze
      80quatre-vingt81quatre-vingtun
      90quatre-vingt-dix91quatre-vingtonze
      100cent101cent un
    • NumberFrench
      22vingt-deux
      23vingt-trois
      24vingt-quatre
      25vingt-cinq
      26vingt-six
      27vingt-sept
      28vingt-huit
      29vingt-neuf
      32trente-deux
      42quarante-deux
      52cinquante-deux
      62soixante-deux
      72soixante-douze
      92quatre-vingt-douze
  • The form chez, like the forms à and de is a preposition and occurs before nouns and pronouns. However, chez occurs only before a noun (or pronoun) which refers to a person, à has no such restriction:
    • FrenchEnglish
      chez (a person)at, to a person's house, home, office, store, place of business, etc.
      à (a place)at, to a place, etc.
    • FrenchEnglish
      Je vais chez le boulanger.I'm going to the baker's.
      Je vais à la boulangerie.I'm going to the bakery.
  • The third of the highly frequent verbs in French is aller (to go).
  • FrenchEnglish
    je vaisI go / I am going
    tu vasyou go / you are going (singular)
    il va (elle,on)he goes / he is going (she, one)
    nous allonswe go / we are going
    vous allezyou go / you are going (plural)
    ils vont (elles)they go / they are going

Lesson 5

  • Indefinite determiners 1 (Indefinite adjectives):
    • Plusieurs (several) is always and only plural.
    • Quelques (a few) also occurs before plural nouns.
    • Chaque (each) is always singular.
    • Certain (certain) shows gender and number.
    • Quel (which, what) shows gender and number.
    • FrenchEnglish
      plusieurs hôtels several hotels
      quelques minutes a few minutes
      chaque auto each car
      certains billets certain tickets
    • Feminine Masculine
      Singular certaine certain
      Plural certaines certains
    • FeminineMasculine
      quelle quel
      quelles quels
  • After the sequence ne + Verb + pas, the determiners un, une, des are replaced by de (or d' if followed by a word beginning with a vowel):
  • AffirmativeNegative
    Il a un livre. Il n'a pas de livre.
    Il a des livres. Il n'a pas de livres.
  • Large cardinal numbers include:
    • 100 cent
      200 deux cents
      300 trois cents
      400 quatre cents
      500 cinq cents
      600 six cents
      700 sept cents
      800 huit cents
      900 neuf cents
      1000 mille
      1100 onze cents ou mille cent
      1200 douze cents ou mille deux cents
      1300 treize cents ou mille trois cents
      1400 quatorze cents ou mille quatre cents
      1500 quinze cents ou mille cinq cents
      1600 seize cents ou mille six cents
      1700 dix-sept cents ou mille sept cents
      1800 dix-huit cents ou mille huit cents
      1900 dix-neuf cents ou mille neuf cents
      2000 deux mille
      3000 trois mille
      10000 dix mille
      20000 vingt mille
      30000 trente mille
      100000 cent mille
      200000 deux cent mille
      300000 trois cent mille
      1000000 un million
      5000000 cinq millions
      1000000000 un milliard
    • The numbers million and milliard are marked nouns:
    • cent francs one hundred francs
      mille francs one thousand francs
      un million de francs one million francs
      un milliard de francs one billion francs
  • Weather phenomena are expressed by verbs having as subjects the impersonal subject pronoun il. There are three such verb patterns:
    • Il fait, followed by:
      • certain adjectives
      • du + Noun
    • Il y a, followed by du/de la + Noun
    • Special verbs (pleuvoir = to rain, geler = to freeze, neiger = to snow)
    • FrenchEnglish
      Il fait froid. It's cold weather.
      Il fait chaud. It's hot weather.
      Il fait sec. It's dry weather.
      Il fait beau (temps). It's nice weather.
      Il fait mauvais (temps). It's bad weather.
      Il fait frais. It's cool weather.
      Il fait du soleil.It's sunny weather.
      Il fait du vent.It's windy weather
      Il y a du soleil. It's sunny weather.
      Il y a du vent. It's windy weather
      Il pleut. It's raining.
      Il gèle. It's freezing.
      Il neige. It's snowing.
  • Here are the forms of the highly frequent verb faire (to do, to make):
    • Subject Pronoun (SP) + Verb (V):
    • FrenchEnglish
      je fais I make
      tu fais you make (singular)
      il fait (elle,on) he makes (she, one)
      nous faisons we make
      vous faîtes you make (plural)
      ils font (elles) they make
    • Verb + SP (in questions):
    • FrenchEnglish
      Est-ce que je fais ...?  do I make …?
      fais-tu ...? do you make ...? (singular)
      fait-il ...? (elle,on) does he make ...? (she,one)
      faisons-nous ...? do we make ...?
      faîtes-vous ...? do you make ...? (plural)
      font-ils ...? (elles) do they make ...?
    • SP + ne + V + pas:
    • FrenchEnglish
      je ne fais pas ...
      		 I don't make ...
      tu ne fais pas  ... you don't make ... (singular)
      il ne fait pas ... (elle, on) he doesn't make ... (she, one)
      nous ne faisons pas ... we don't make ...
      vous ne faîtes pas ... you don't make ... (plural)
      ils ne font pas ... (elles) they don't make ...
    • ne + V + SP + pas?:
    • FrenchEnglish
      Est-ce que je ne fais pas de café? don't I make coffee?
      ne fais-tu pas ... ? don't you make ...? (singular)
      ne fait-il pas ...? (elle, on) doesn't he make ...? (she, one)
      ne faisons-nous pas ...? don't we make ...?
      ne faîtes-vous pas ...? don't you make...? (plural)
      ne font-ils pas ...? (elles) don't they make ...?

Lesson 7

  • Adjectives: words that modify nouns or pronouns:
    • Number:
      • Plural of most French adjectives is formed by adding an -s to the singular form.
      • Adjectives ending in s or x in the singular do not change in the plural.
      • Adjectives ending in eau in the masculine singular add an x to form the masculine plural.
      • SingularPlural
        le petit magasinles petits magasins
        la grande routeles grandes routes
        le vieux magasin (old)les vieux magasins
        le mauvais hôtelles mauvais hôtels
        le nouveau magasinles nouveaux magasins
    • Gender:
      • In general the feminine form is obtained by adding an e to the masculine form (unless it already ends in an e in the masculine form).
      • Adjectives ending in é follow the general rule and add an e in the feminine form.
      • MasculineFeminine
        un petit paquetune petite valise
        un jeune employéune jeune employée
        un autre boulangerune autre pâtisserie
      • Ordinal numbers are formed by adding ième to the cardinal numbers.
      • Masculine / Masculin Feminine / Féminin
        le deuxième jour la deuxième semaine
        le troisième matin la troisième fois
        le quarante-cinquième mot la quarante-cinquième question
    • Special cases:
      • When the masculine form ends in x, the feminine form will end in se.
      • When the masculine form ends in ier, the feminine form will end in ière.
      • When the masculine form ends in f, the feminine form will end in ve.
      • MasculineFeminine
        Il est heureuxElle est heureuse
        le premier étagela première semaine
        le dernier jourla dernière année
        un manteau neufune robe neuve
      • Some adjectives have two shapes for the masculine singular: one is used before words beginning with a vowel, the other, before words beginning with a consonant.
      • MasculineFeminine
        SingularPluralSingularPlural
        Before a vowelBefore a consonant
        vieilvieuxvieuxvieillevieilles
        belbeaubeauxbellebelles
        nouvelnouveaunouveauxnouvellenouvelles
    • Exceptions:
    • MasculineFeminine
      un costume blancune chemise blanche
      un temps fraisune soirée fraîche
      un climat secune saison sèche
      un costume marronune robe marron
      un gros livre une grosse caisse
      un ancien hôtelune ancienne route
      un bon pâtissierune bonne boulangerie
  • Object pronouns precede the verb in affirmative, negative or interrogative sentences:
    • FrenchEnglish
      me, m'me, to me
      nousus, to us
      vousyou, to you
      le, l'him, it
      la, l'her, it
      lesthem
    • Elle me convient tout à fait.
    • Combien vous dois-je?
    • Je la prends.
    • Pourquoi ne l'envoyez-vous pas de chez vous?
  • Ce occurs only as a third person subject of the verb être and can be translated by 'it', 'he', 'she' , 'they', 'that'. Ce is used instead of 'il, elle, etc.' if the verb 'être' is followed by either:
    • A noun marker (le, mon, etc.): C'est un très bon restaurant.
    • A pronoun: C'est tout ce qui reste.
    • A clause: C'est exactement ce qu'il me faut.
  • Question-words:
    • FrenchEnglish
      qui?who?
      qu', que?what?
      où?where?
      quand?when?
      pourquoi?why?
      comment?how?
      combien?how much, how many?
      quel, quelle?which, what?
    • The question-words are followed by subject-verb inversion, whether the subject is a noun or a pronoun.
    • Examples: Structure:
      Comment va votre frère? Q + Verb + Noun
      Comment va-t-il? Q + Verb + SP
    • The question-word combien may occur before a noun; in this position it has the form combien de.
      • Combien vous dois-je?
      • Combien de malles avez-vous?

Lesson 8

  • Adjective Positions: In French, some adjectives:
    • Follow the noun (post-nominal).
    • une valise verte
      un employé français
    • Precede the noun (pre-nominal).
    • Type of adjectives Example
      demonstrative adjectives ces enfants
      indefinite adjectives plusieurs enfants
      interrogative adjectives quelle heure
      possessive adjectives ma soeur
      numeral adjectives deux enfants
      ordinal adjectives premier étage
    • Some adjectives can occasionally change their position for stylistic reasons.
    • Some adjectives are used in different positions according to the nouns they qualify.
    • French / Français English / Anglais
      d'excellentes vacances excellent vacation
      des vacances excellentes excellent vacation
      la prochaine fois next time
      la semaine prochaine next week
    • Some adjectives have different meanings according to their position:
      • AdjectivesPre-nominalPost-nominal
        seul only one alone
        cher dear expensive
        ancien former old, ancient
        vrai real true
        dernier last of a series last, preceding
  • 'En' replaces 'de + noun or pronoun':
    • The object pronoun en is used instead of 'le, la, les' if the noun it replaces is used in an indefinite or partitive sense:
      • Voilà des billets. Here are some tickets.
        En voilà. Here are some.
        Voilà les billets. Here are the tickets.
        Les voilà. Here they are.
        Il achète du pain. He is buying some bread.
        Il en achète. He is buying some.
        Il achète le pain. He is buying the bread.
        Il l' achète He is buying it.
    • The object pronoun en is also used when the noun is preceded by a number or an adverb of quantity:
      • Donnez-moi quatre chemises. Give me four shirts.
        Donnez-m'en quatre. Give me four.
        Nous avons plusieurs chambres libres. We have several rooms vacant.
        Nous en avons plusieurs de libres. We have several vacant.
    • En can be used for 'de + location', meaning from there:
      • Il vient du Midi. He is coming from the south of France.
        Il en vient. He is coming from there.
        Il part du bureau à 6 heures. He leaves the office at 6 o'clock.
        Il en part à 6 heures. He leaves it at 6 o'clock.
    • En is used with verbs followed by 'de', even if the noun or pronoun is used in a definite way:
      • Il a besoin de l'auto. He needs the car.
        Il en a besoin. He needs it.
        Il a besoin de celles-ci. He needs these.
        Il en a besoin. He needs them.
        Elle parle du discours. She's talking about the speech.
        Elle en parle. She's talking about it.
  • The object pronoun y is invariable. It precedes the verb, except in the imperative affirmative:
    • Y replaces 'à + noun or pronoun'
    • French / Français English / Anglais
      Il travaille à la gare. He works at the station.
      Il y travaille. He works there.
      Est-il au bureau? Is he at the office?
      Y est-il? Is he there?
    • Y could be translated by 'of or about it' or 'of or about them', but should only refer to an animal or an inanimate object:
    • French / Français English / Anglais
      Nous pensons aux vacances. We think about the vacation.
      Nous y pensons. We think about it.
      Nous pensons à la neige. We’re thinking about the snow.
      Nous y pensons. We’re thinking about it.
  • Verb forms in ez occurring initially and neither preceded nor followed by the subject pronoun 'vous' are used to indicate a request or a command.
  • FrenchEnglish
    Vous parlez français. You're speaking French. / You speak French
    Parlez français. Speak French.
  • Similarly, ons forms in the same frame are regarded as imperatives, with the meaning 'Let's ...'
  • FrenchEnglish
    Parlons français. Let's speak French.
    Allons dans le Midi. Let's go to the south of France.
  • Four verbs have special imperative stems:
    • FrenchEnglishFrenchEnglish
      avoir to have Ayez de la monnaie. Have change.
      Ayons un enfant. Let's have a child
      être to be Soyez en avance. Be early.
      Soyons fous. Let's be crazy. /
      Let's go wild.
      savoir to know Sachez ce que vous voulez. Know what you want.
      Sachons dire non. Let's say "no"
      vouloir to want Veuillez me suivre. Please follow me.
      Veuillons  (form rarely used)
  • Object pronouns follow affirmative imperatives and precede negative imperatives:
    • FrenchEnglish
      Parlez-lui ce soir. Speak to him tonight.
      Ne lui parlez pas ce soir. Don't speak to him tonight.
      Donnons-lui le numéro. Let's give him the number.
      Ne lui donnons pas le numéro. Let's not give him the number.

From \"optilingo.com/blog/french/french-pronouns/\"

Personal Pronouns

  • Subject Reflexive Direct Object Indirect Object Prepositional
    Je (I) Me (myself) Me (me) Me (me) Moi (me)
    Tu (you informal) Te (yourself, informal) Te (you, informal) Te (you, informal) Toi (you, informal)
    Il (he) Se (himself) Le (him) Lui (him) Lui (him)
    Elle (she) Se (herself) La (her) Lui (her) Elle (her)
    Nous (we) Nous (ourselves) Nous (us) Nous (us) Nous (us)
    Vous (plural you or formal singular you) Vous (yourselves or yourself, formal) Vous (plural you or formal singular you) Vous (plural you or formal singular you) Vous (plural you or formal formal)
    Ils (they) Se (themselves) Les (them) Leur (them) Eux (them)
    Elles (they) Se (themselves) Les (them) Leur (them) Elles (them)
  • Reflexive Pronouns: You use these when the action reflects back on you. Je me brosse les dents - I brush my teeth (for myself).
  • Direct object pronouns in French represent the things or persons affected directly by the verb's action. Il la voit - He sees her.
  • Indirect object pronouns replace people who are indirectly affected by the action. Tu m'as donné un chat - You gave me a cat.

Impersonal Pronouns

  • Adverbial Pronouns: en, y
    • \"Y\" replaces \"à + location\"
    • En replaced \"de + indefinite article + noun\"
    • EnglishFrench
      Je vais à la piscine.I'm going to the pool.
      J'y vais.I'm going there.
      Tu as du sucre?Do you have some sugar?
      Oui, j'en ai.Yes, I have some.
  • Demonstrative Pronouns:
  • Masculine SingularFeminine SingularMasculine PluralFeminine Plural
    CeluiCelleCeuxCelles
  • Indefinite Demonstrative Pronouns (ce, ceci, cela, ça):
    • You use indefinite demonstrative pronouns to refer to an unknown noun.
    • Ceci n'est pas une pipe - This is not a pipe.
  • Impersonal Subjects (ce, il):
    • If the subjects of sentences are people, you use personal pronouns. But, if they're object, you use \"ce\" or \"il\" to introduce impersonal verb or expression.
    • J'ai jeté un liver; il a volé - I threw a book; it flew.
  • Indefinite Pronouns (autre, certains, plusieurs, ...): You use indefinite pronouns to indicate an unknown quantity or description. You can translate these as \"other\", \"certain\", \"many\" in English.
  • Interrogative pronouns: Qui a gagné le jeu - Who won the game?
  • Negative Pronouns: ne __ personne, ne __ rien ...
  • Possessive pronouns:
    • Possessive AdjectiveMasculineFemininePlural
      My Mon Ma Mes
      Your (informal) Ton Ta Tes
      His/Her/Its Son Sa Ses
      Our Notre Notre Nos
      Your (plural or singular formal) Votre Votre Vos
      Their Leur Leur Leurs
    • Your address - votre adresse
    • Her passport - son passeport
  • Relative Pronouns (qui, que, dont, où, lequel):
    • These pronouns can link different parts of the sentence together.
    • Nous allons là où nous avons passé notre lune de miel - We're going where we spent our honeymoon.
  • Indefinite Relative Pronouns (ce qui, ce que, ce dont, ce à quoi):
    • Similar to the previous type of pronoun, but instead, the thing it replaces is unknown.
    • Ce que tu veux ne peut pas arriver - What you want can't happen.
"; // Exports export default code;
